meta.name: Altered Mental Status — Mixed Etiology Workup (UTI, Polypharmacy, Hypoglycemia)

patient.name: Wendell Hopkins
patient.chiefComplaint: Family found him confused, lethargic, and 'not himself' this morning

history.hpi: 65-year-old male with extensive comorbidities was found by his daughter this morning sitting on the edge of his bed, confused, slow to respond, and unable to recognize her at first. Daughter says he was 'normal' last night when she said goodnight. Yesterday afternoon he complained of urinary frequency and burning with urination but refused to go to the doctor. He has glyburide, oxycodone (recently increased for back pain), gabapentin, and several other medications. The pill organizer for today is empty (he may have taken meds early this morning before becoming confused). No reported witnessed seizure, no fall, no chest pain. Daughter denies alcohol or illicit drug use. Daughter denies head trauma. Patient is intermittently responsive but cannot give a coherent history.
pastMedical: Chronic low back pain (recently increased oxycodone)
medications: Oxycodone 10 mg PO every 4 hours PRN (recently up-titrated)
history.lastOralIntake: Half a sandwich at dinner last night; nothing today per daughter
history.events: Daughter found patient confused and lethargic in bedroom this morning, unable to recognize her at first. Yesterday's urinary symptoms suggest possible UTI; recent oxycodone uptitration; missed meal with sulfonylurea on board create competing differentials.

scene.dispatch: Dispatch: 65-year-old male, altered mental status, family states 'not himself.' Single-family home.
scene.location: Single-family home, master bedroom on the main floor. Adequate lighting; clear path for stretcher.
scene.safety: Scene secure. Daughter present and cooperative. No environmental hazards. No firearms or weapons visible.
scene.bystanders: Daughter (primary historian) lives next door and checks on him daily. Reports patient was 'normal' last night, complained of urinary burning and frequency yesterday, and was found confused this morning. Brings the medication list and pill organizer. Notes the urine in the bathroom toilet looks 'cloudy and dark.'
visualCues: Patient sitting on the edge of the bed, head down, slow to look up when spoken to
Pill organizer on nightstand (today's compartment empty; tomorrow's full)
Bottle of oxycodone on nightstand with recent fill date and lower-than-expected pill count
Bathroom door open with cloudy, dark yellow urine visible in the toilet
Patient's bed sheets damp, suggesting urinary incontinence overnight
No needle tracks, no medication patches, no overdose paraphernalia
No CO detector visible in the home; furnace running

phases.name: Initial Contact
phases.description: EMS finds a 65-year-old male with multiple competing causes of altered mental status: urinary symptoms suggesting UTI/sepsis, sulfonylurea use with a missed meal (hypoglycemia), recent opioid up-titration (overdose), and baseline dementia. The case is built to teach the AEIOU-TIPS systematic workup. This is the initial contact phase: dispatch, SAMPLE history, scene findings, and a structured AMS evaluation drive the response.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent and self-maintained; no snoring, no obstruction
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Shallow, slightly slow but adequate respirations; clear bilateral lung sounds; no wheezing or rales
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Strong but rapid radial pulse; warm flushed skin; cap refill 2 sec
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Moves all four extremities; no focal weakness; tracks finger inconsistently; slow to respond to commands
otherFindings: No external signs of head trauma
Mild suprapubic discomfort with light palpation
Patient confused: knows his name but not the year or where he is
Daughter reports baseline mild dementia but says 'this is way worse'
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: Slurred, slow: 'Who are you... where's Linda... what time is it...'

expectedActions.shortAction: Primary survey ABC; ensure patent airway, support as needed
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: With depressed mental status, initial focus is on airway protection, oxygenation, ventilation, and perfusion.
expectedActions.rationale: Always first; AMS patients can lose airway quickly.

expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Notes/Educational Pearls: Consider the following differential using the mnemonic AEIOU-TIPS: A – Alcohol, Abuse, Atypical migraine; E – Epilepsy, Electrolytes; I – Insulin (hypoglycemia); O – Oxygen, Overdose; U – Uremia (kidney failure); T – Trauma, Tumor; I – Infection; P – Psych, Poisoning; S – Seizure, Subarachnoid hemorrhage, Sepsis.
expectedActions.rationale: Forces a structured workup so multiple competing causes (hypoglycemia, sepsis, opioid effect) all get addressed.

expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Treatment and Interventions: Glucose. Performance Measures: Hypoglycemia considered and treated appropriately. Blood glucose level obtained.
expectedActions.rationale: Easiest and most rapidly correctable cause; must be done first on every AMS.

expectedActions.shortAction: D10% 100 mL IV (10 g) — preferred concentration; reassess
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Unconscious patient, or patients who are unable to protect their own airway: Dextrose IV – administer in incremental doses until mental status improves or maximum field dosing is reached (if available, D10% is preferred). Maximum field adult dosing: 25 g of 10–50% dextrose IV. 50 mL of 50% dextrose. 100 mL of 25% dextrose. 250 mL of 10% dextrose.
expectedActions.rationale: D10 in incremental doses avoids overshoot hyperglycemia and venous tissue damage from D50.

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize sulfonylurea (glyburide) — recurrence likely; mandatory transport
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Sulfonylureas (e.g., glyburide, glipizide) have long half-lives ranging from 12–60 hrs. Patients with corrected hypoglycemia who are taking these agents are at particular risk for recurrent symptoms and frequently require hospital admission.
expectedActions.rationale: Even if dextrose 'fixes' AMS, sulfonylurea half-life dictates inpatient observation; refusal of transport is unsafe.

expectedActions.shortAction: Consider naloxone if respiratory depression (RR <12, SpO2 falling, miosis)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If the patient has respiratory depression from a confirmed or suspected opioid overdose, consider naloxone administration. The administration of the initial dose or subsequent doses can be incrementally titrated until respiratory depression is reversed. Adults: The typical initial adult dose ranges between Naloxone 0.4–2 mg IV, IM, up to a dose of 4 mg IN or 5 mg ETT.
expectedActions.rationale: Recent opioid uptitration is on the differential; naloxone is therapeutic, not diagnostic. Use only with respiratory depression.

expectedActions.shortAction: Do NOT give naloxone as a diagnostic 'shotgun' if breathing is adequate
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Performance Measures: Naloxone is used as therapeutic intervention, not a diagnostic tool.
expectedActions.rationale: Common error; precipitates withdrawal and complicates the picture without reversing AMS in this case.

expectedActions.shortAction: Establish large-bore IV access for fluids, glucose, meds
expectedActions.rationale: Required for D10 administration and any sepsis bolus.

expectedActions.shortAction: Take temperature (finds 38.7°C — fever; sepsis on differential)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: Temperature was taken when able.
expectedActions.rationale: Easy to miss in a confused elderly patient; fever + urinary symptoms = consider urosepsis.

expectedActions.shortAction: 12-lead EKG to evaluate for cardiac dysrhythmia/MI as cause
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Treatment and Interventions: EKG or cardiac monitor: arrhythmia limiting perfusion.
expectedActions.rationale: Silent MI in elderly diabetics is a known AMS cause; EKG is fast and high-yield.

expectedActions.shortAction: Survey medications, pill organizer, environment for clues
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Environment: Survey for pills, paraphernalia, substance use, medication patches, medical devices, ambient temperature, social indicators of neglect, carbon monoxide exposures, multiple casualties with same complaint.
expectedActions.rationale: Pill organizer state, oxycodone bottle, urinary symptoms together construct the differential.

transitions.condition: Crew administers D10% IV. Glucose normalizes and mental status improves somewhat, but patient remains lethargic and febrile — pointing toward a second cause (sepsis from suspected UTI). Crew expands the differential and continues workup.
Crew corrects glucose, AMS improves transiently, and crew stops investigating. Sulfonylurea recurrence and underlying urosepsis are missed; if a refusal is offered, this is the unsafe outcome.

phases.name: D10 given; second cause identified
phases.description: After D10%, glucose rises to 96 mg/dL and the patient is more responsive but still lethargic and warm to touch. Repeat temperature confirms 38.6°C. Tachycardia and borderline hypotension persist. Crew recognizes this is a multi-hit picture (corrected hypoglycemia + likely urosepsis) and begins fluid resuscitation while maintaining the AMS workup.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew administered D10%, glucose corrected to 96 mg/dL, and recognized that the patient remains symptomatic despite glucose correction — pointing to additional causes (fever, tachycardia, urinary findings = urosepsis).

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Tachycardic regular pulse, warm flushed skin, cap refill 2 sec
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Moves all extremities; following commands now
otherFindings: Patient now oriented to person and place but not to time
Reports burning with urination on direct questioning
Skin still warm with mild diaphoresis
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: 'I... I think I'm at home? Yeah, my daughter — where's my daughter?'

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize sepsis: fever + tachycardia + low BP + AMS source
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Performance Measures: Sepsis considered as a possible cause of hypotension. Hypotension appropriately treated.
expectedActions.rationale: Sepsis is now the second active hit; failure to recognize will under-resuscitate.

expectedActions.shortAction: NS 500 mL bolus; reassess; up to 30 mL/kg if not improved
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Adult: Physiologic target: MAP goal 65 mmHg. Fluid goal of up to 30 mL/kg of isotonic fluid by administering rapid, predetermined boluses (e.g., 500 mL) unless the MAP goal is achieved, or pulmonary edema develops.
expectedActions.rationale: Septic shock fluid resuscitation per MATC Shock guideline.

expectedActions.shortAction: Trend EtCO2 (low values reflect poor perfusion); lactate if available
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Pulse oximetry and EtCO2 (reading of less than 25 mmHg may be sign of poor perfusion). Check lactate, if available (greater than 2 mmol/L is abnormal).
expectedActions.rationale: Both are sensitive indicators of occult shock and treatment response.

expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Disposition: If hypoglycemia with continued symptoms, transport to closest appropriate receiving facility.
expectedActions.rationale: Even if mental status appears improved, this patient cannot safely refuse transport.

expectedActions.shortAction: Early notification: AMS, hypoglycemia + suspected urosepsis
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Provide advanced notification to the hospital.
expectedActions.rationale: Pre-arrival activation primes ED for rapid antibiotics, blood cultures, and ICU consideration.

phases.name: Anchored on hypoglycemia
phases.description: Crew gave dextrose, mental status improved transiently, and the crew accepted that as the entire diagnosis without continuing the workup. Patient is offered the option to refuse transport. Returning to baseline glucose has not addressed the underlying urosepsis or the sulfonylurea recurrence risk; in 30-60 minutes the patient will be hypoglycemic again, and meanwhile the urosepsis is progressing. This is the teaching branch: anchoring bias kills.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew accepted hypoglycemia as the sole cause of AMS, did not check temperature or pursue the urinary symptom history, and did not enforce the sulfonylurea-mandates-transport rule.

otherFindings: Patient and daughter discussing refusal of transport
Crew has not taken a temperature or asked about urinary symptoms
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: 'I'm fine now. Just need to eat something. I'm not going to the hospital.'

expectedActions.shortAction: Take temperature, ask about urinary symptoms, complete the AEIOU-TIPS
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Notes/Educational Pearls: Consider the following differential using the mnemonic AEIOU-TIPS: A – Alcohol, Abuse, Atypical migraine; E – Epilepsy, Electrolytes; I – Insulin (hypoglycemia); O – Oxygen, Overdose; U – Uremia (kidney failure); T – Trauma, Tumor; I – Infection; P – Psych, Poisoning; S – Seizure, Subarachnoid hemorrhage, Sepsis.
expectedActions.rationale: Recovery action — the structured differential explicitly catches infection and AEIOU-TIPS as a second hit.

expectedActions.shortAction: Refuse to accept refusal — sulfonylurea + abnormal vitals = transport
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Sulfonylureas (e.g., glyburide, glipizide) have long half-lives ranging from 12–60 hrs. Patients with corrected hypoglycemia who are taking these agents are at particular risk for recurrent symptoms and frequently require hospital admission. Disposition: If symptoms of hypoglycemia resolve after treatment, release without transport should only be considered if all the following are true: ... Patient takes insulin or metformin to control diabetes and does not take long-acting oral sulphunylurea agents (e.g., glipizide, glyburide, or others); ... No major co-morbid symptoms exist, like chest pain, shortness of breath, seizures, intoxication.
expectedActions.rationale: Refusal criteria explicitly exclude sulfonylureas; this is the most common AMS-related medical-legal error.

expectedActions.shortAction: If refusal sought, document capacity assessment, risks, family involvement
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Disposition: If symptoms of hypoglycemia resolve after treatment, release without transport should only be considered if all the following are true: ... Patient or legal guardian refuses transport and EMS clinicians agree transport not indicated; A reliable adult will be staying with patient; No major co-morbid symptoms exist; A clear cause of the hypoglycemia is identified.
expectedActions.rationale: Even if the team accepts a refusal, the protocol's checklist must be documented and met — and in this scenario it can't be.
